old_claim.note: Dynamo provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from electric_motor to dynamo; Faraday's 1821 electromagnetic-rotation motor predates the local dynamo/electric-generator node and uses current plus magnetism rather than a generator as a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Royal Institution account of Faraday's 3 September 1821 electromagnetic-rotation experiment producing the first electric motor.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source dates the first electric motor to Faraday's 1821 current-and-magnet apparatus.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A 1821 motor cannot depend on the graph's dynamo/electric-generator capability, now dated to Faraday's 1831 generator work; the source supports the motor's earlier chronology and mechanism.

ontology_before: Electric motors appeared downstream of dynamos, implying generated electrical power was needed before Faraday's motor principle.
ontology_after: Electric motors depend on electricity and early electromagnetism; dynamos are a later generation technology and can support later power systems without being a motor prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electric_motor no longer directly depends on dynamo and remains connected through electricity, construction, and electromagnetism_early_discoveries.
would_reject_if: A primary historical source showed Faraday's 1821 motor required an electric generator.
The electric_motor node were re-scoped to commercial factory motors powered by generated electricity rather than the first motor principle.
The dynamo node were re-scoped to pre-1821 battery or current-supply technology instead of electric generators.
short_reason: Faraday's motor predates dynamos and does not require a generator.
